content:
I was looking through the rust drivers currently in the Linux kernel. I noticed that some use InPlaceModule (like rnull) and some use Module (like rust_minimal). What is the criteria one would use to choose between the two?

In particular, I was experimenting with making it possible to define the number of devices that rnull creates. My plan was to base on Andreas' work to add module params and to change the disk attr in the NullBlkModule struct to be a KVec instead of the single object and change the init to create the objects in a loop.

My try is here: https://github.com/wt/linux/commit/9558d059b1f36b5c98e39af5c63f4ff614412124

I am trying to figure out if I need to change to use Module instead of InPlaceModule since each device requires an allocation for the KVec because of the push. I am wondering this because I see rust_minimal using Module and using a KVec attr.

Hi!

Wren T said:

In particular, I was experimenting with making it possible to define the number of devices that rnull creates. My plan was to base on Andreas' work to add module params and to change the disk attr in the NullBlkModule struct to be a KVec instead of the single object and change the init to create the objects in a loop.

This work is already done, see downstream rnull tree. Patches are on list for creating devices at runtime via configfs. For setting the number of devices created at module init time, I am waiting for the module parameter series to merge.
The patches enabling device creation via configfs are here.
Regarding InPlaceModule, you would use that if you need to in-place-initialization of your module struct. rnull needs to pin both of the fields in NullBlkModule, hence the use of InPlaceModule.

If you look at rnull in the downstream tree you will see this:

#[pin_data]
struct NullBlkModule {
    #[pin]
    configfs_subsystem: kernel::configfs::Subsystem<configfs::Config>,
    #[pin]
    param_disks: Mutex<KVec<GenDisk<NullBlkDevice>>>,
}

@Andreas Hindborg I figured you were probably working on this. I was just trying to get comfortable with some basic rusty stuff in the kernel. Thanks for pointing out your work.

I'm glad I picked the right type. I am looking at your work right now.
@Andreas Hindborg On the Module vs InPlaceModule, should I always prefer InPlaceModule? Are there ever times that it cannot be used?
I think it can always be used, but it adds a bit of complexity. So if you don't need it, you can just go with the regular module trait.
Using InPlaceModule and Module is exactly equivalent when both are possible, but sometimes only InPlaceModule is possible.
Maybe we should get rid of the distinction and only have a Module trait that uses in-place?
Idk, the Module trait is a bit more simple. No reason to return in place initialization function if not required.
When is it required? I tried to find information about this. Is this something that is rust specific?
It's required when any fields of your module require in-place initialization.
Does a Mutex require that because references to it can be used outside the module?
Apologies if these are dumb questions.
It is because a Mutex requires initialization before it is safe to use. If you just create a Mutex without initializing it, you may end up dereferencing null pointers.

As far as I recall, one of the reasons is that a Mutex may contain a linked list. An empty kernel linked list must have the next and previous fields set to point to point to itself to be valid. The default zero value of those pointers are not a valid value.

No, a mutex requires in-place initialization because it contains a list_head for the list of waiting threads, and the default value of a list_head is that the next and prev pointers point at the list_head itself. Therefore, initializing a mutex requires knowing the address it will be stored at.
Bottom line is that this structure requires initialization to happen before it is safe to use.
PinInit allows us to do that.
That makes sense, so the Mutex is the reason for the InPlaceModule in rnull. Would most non-trivial modules need a mutex like this?
I don't know. Maybe? Time will tell.

I read about the pin-init. Seems reasonable.

If I tried to do something stupid like create a mutex in a Module, would the rust type system be smart enough to stop me from doing that?
It would not compile.
That's pretty nice. Is there any way to make an InPlaceModule fail to compile if it could be implemented with a Module instead? Seems like InPlaceModules could be used when it isn't necessary.
No because there could be fields where in-place initialization is optional.
For example, a large array of zeros should probably be initialized with in-place-init to avoid having the compiler place the large array on the stack, but the language does not require it.

Alice Ryhl said:

For example, a large array of zeros should probably be initialized with in-place-init to avoid having the compiler place the large array on the stack, but the language does not require it.

You might crash the kernel with a stack overflow though.
Would fields having optional in-place init able to be initted with {,try_}pin_init, or does that kind of complication require the use of pin_init_from_closure?
You can use the macros.
Are there any examples of such a thing in the kernel today?
Just so I can see how that works.

BTW, @Andreas Hindborg I really think the API you're working on for kernel params is so much cleaner than the C api. I just wanted to say I really like your work.

And thank both (@Andreas Hindborg and @Alice Ryhl) of you for taking the time to answer some of these questions. Y'all rock.

Wren T said:

BTW, @Andreas Hindborg I really think the API you're working on for kernel params is so much cleaner than the C api. I just wanted to say I really like your work.

Thanks! It's really original work by @Adam Bratschi-Kaye (he) and then the feedback loop of the mailing list that got us here.
First commit of this by Adam was 2021. We will get there eventually.

Another question: on the linux next docs for try_pin_init, I see the following: "The syntax is identical to pin_init! with the following exception: you must append ? $type after the struct initializer to specify the error type you want to use."

However, I do not see that ? Error syntax used in the try_pin_init use in the rnull.rs file.

The docs sound like the ? Error syntax at the end of the initializer needs to be there, but it actually appears to be optional. Is that a bug in the docs, or am I just reading that really poorly?
that syntax is indeed optional and the docs just have become stale, feel free to submit a patch to fix that :)

@Benno Lossin I see the macro definition here. The ? Error sytax doesn't look optional. However, I am not good a reading rust macro syntax. I am clearly not reading that correctly. Can you help me understand why that syntax is not required?
Ah yes, the one defined in pin-init doesn't have the optional syntax
but the one in the kernel crate does: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/kernel/init.rs#L290-L305
and there the docs also say it's optional, so all is fine :)
Ah, snap! I thought that was the kernel one. Oops.
linked from here: https://rust-for-linux.com/pin-init
yeah pin-init is being used in the kernel, see: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/tree/rust-next/rust/pin-init
but the kernel also defines its own try_pin_init! macro to default to the kernel's own Error type

Andreas Hindborg said:

Thanks! It's really original work by Adam Bratschi-Kaye (he) and then the feedback loop of the mailing list that got us here.

First commit of this by Adam was 2021. We will get there eventually.

It originally came from https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/7, which I think was actually the second PR in the project. :)

I introduced that syntax in that PR, and also it was also where the proc macro crate appeared:

params: {
    my_parameter: i64 {
        default: 1,
        description: "This parameter has a default of 1",
    },
},
@Miguel Ojeda I think it's so much more readable.
Then Adam immediately started adding support for more parameter features, which was great -- he was one of the very first contributors.

Wren T said:

Miguel Ojeda I think it's so much more readable.

Thanks, glad you like it!
@Benno Lossin Should that page link to the one used in the kernel instead of the one on Rust-for-Linux github?
no, you probably found the only instance of a possible source for confusion :)
the issue is that I don't want to use a different name for try_pin_init in pin-init, since that is the natural name. and the kernel also shouldn't use a different one...

what we could do would be to add some docs describing the pattern used in the kernel (ie create a new macro with the same name and then add a default error) and then also say that this is what's done in the kernel
That all makes sense.
@Andreas Hindborg It would be nice mentioning a bit of the history of the code in the commit messages themselves (they are fairly empty in v13 anyway).

I'm so amazed how far this has come without param support. I was in disbelief when I found it didn't exist for rust. I was super pleased to see the patches on the ML. That was kinda my first kinda research into the rust for linux code.

Yeah, originally I think I added it because parameters are sometimes useful for testing things here and there, and it was also a way for me to play around adding proc macro support which I assumed we would need sooner or later anyway.

But for actual upstreaming one needs a use case, and generally one nowadays tries to avoid parameters unless there is no other way of doing something, so it wouldn't get merged for a long time.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Andreas Hindborg It would be nice mentioning a bit of the history of the code in the commit messages themselves (they are fairly empty in v13 anyway).

Good idea! You seem to have a good grasp on that, can I get you to contribute a paragraph? Then I'll include it in the next iteration.
I mean, I don't know exactly from where you took each part/commit or what changes were done, but I am happy to help on the early part, sure.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I mean, I don't know exactly from where you took each part/commit or what changes were done, but I am happy to help on the early part, sure.

That would be great, thanks.
